viewingNote.zh: 我走向车旁，打开盒子，拿出那尊迟 迟没有拍摄的上师石雕造像 。说 道：“这次我们还有这件上师像一直没拍照，我想，最适合的时间出现了。那就 是此刻你散发的光 。质朴、鲜活、细腻 、又充满力量 。”扎堆面带羞涩的，双 手接过造像，轻轻举过头顶，低声念了一串经文 。“感觉这位上师的头好圆。” 扎堆笑着说 。我点头：“是啊，上师生前就是这个模样。祂的五官量感很大，特 别是眼睛，画得清晰细致，眼白、瞳孔 、瞳仁分明，给人一种强烈的真实感 。 眉毛纤细修长，嘴唇略施红彩。整张面孔因写实而格外生动，仿佛有血有肉。 同样，也正是因为祂五官在整个头部的量感，所以我们第一眼看上去就会细究 祂的整个表情，就是整个人物细微的这种动态 。”我指着雕像继续道：“你再看 祂的背光，虽然厚重且装饰繁复，但第一眼总是先落在祂脸上——那种神 情，好像一汪泉水 ，干净、清澈，透着喜悦与信念，像是智慧沉淀后自然流露 的通透和自信。” 扎堆凝视着雕像，沉思后缓缓说：“祂看起来很慈悲、很简单，但又有种我 说不清楚的能量包裹着。”“从正面看，平易近人；从侧面看，又很庄严。能感 觉到匠人在挑选石头时，就已经开始在想如何挑选石料才能更好的还原自己对 上师的理解和设想 。在雕凿过程中，匠人有在‘寻石造像’，从上师塑造的体量 和整个背光以及底座的和谐度和贴合度来看，匠人在开凿的过程中，像是极为 克制和小心，想尽量寻石来保留人物的量感和气场。” 我继续介绍：“你看这背光，外圈编织纹理像是麻绳，六拏具上的神兽神态 灵动，底座上刻着宝瓶 ，还有一群像童话中走出的大象，整件作品在繁复中透 出一种庄严与灵性 。六拏具上的神兽和底座上的大象都像童话中走出来般，带 着精灵之气 。底座中间雕刻着宝瓶，两侧为布满花纹的雕刻，使雕像整体在繁 复中凸显着一种华贵、具象的场域 。以红、黄色调为主的绘彩，颜色既明亮又 沉稳，凸显着上师的高贵。所以整体就会给人一种庄严的气场和情绪张力”扎 堆听得入神，忽然认真地说：“这尊雕像，像极了我们藏族人的性格。”我一 愣：“怎么说？”他笑了笑：“石头是粗糙坚硬的，但被雕刻后却能展现出温柔、生 动的神态，就像我们藏族人，看起来粗犷、不精致，嘴巴也不利索，但心里，其 实很柔软 。是一种拙中带细、不拘小节的质朴。”我轻声回应：“是啊，外在豁 达洒脱 ，内心细腻澄明 。你也一样——明亮、美好。” 话匣子被打开的扎堆忽然严肃起来：“姐，你知道吗？我们藏族人不害怕死 亡，因为我们相信轮回。”“曾经在纽约有一个以藏文化为主的博物馆——鲁 宾，博物馆曾经有做过一个展，展的主题——死亡不是终结”我回忆道 。扎堆 点头：“我们修法、念经、供灯、转山，这些看似重复的行为，其实是在不断提 醒自己：保持正念，成为一个善良的人 。因为这个世界太吵闹了，我们需要反 复地修习，才不会迷失 。”他顿了顿，语气缓慢而坚定：“如果一个人一生心念是 善的，哪怕身体消失了，在下一个循环 、轮回中，那这个善根就牵引着你去到美 好的地方。“我补充说：“在物理学中，这叫‘能量守恒定律’。能量不会凭空消 失，它只会转换形态 。从这个角度看，我们从未真正离开，只是以另一种形式 继续存在 。死亡和新生共同循环 、共生共存”扎堆忽然调皮地说：“那说不定 这些雕像里，也藏着某些能量呢？”我看着那尊静默的上师，默默微笑 。 是啊。每一个生命走过的痕迹，都会被这个宇宙悄然记录下。肉体终将归 于尘土，但精神会以另一种形态——思想、记忆 、意志 、或一尊雕塑——继续 存在下去 ... 机舱内传来婴儿尖锐而剧烈的啼哭，像初春突至的惊雷，穿透密闭的空 气 ，在耳边炸开。我忽然从梦境中惊醒，意识从虚浮而沉静的朦胧中挣脱 。眼 前依旧是机舱内柔的灯光和陌生的人，低语与呼吸交错。我花了一些时间，才 将身体与意识重新对齐。哭声依旧在持续 。可不知为什么，刚开始觉得刺耳的 声音，在完全清醒之后，竟开始在内心有了一种不同的回响 。那像是生命初生 的召唤，干净、原始、毫无遮掩，像是某种久违的“开始”。 我们出生时本自具足，没有“匮乏”或“需求”的概念，柔软、澄澈、无所 欠缺 。这是一种存在本身的圆满，就像一滴水 ，它已是水本身，不需成为海洋 才能成全自己 。然而随着成长，我们渐渐失去了这份圆满。我们开始“区 分”，世界被划分为对立的两极：好与坏 、有与无 、成功与失败、我与他人 ... 从一体到分裂，我们在学会表达的同时，也开始误以为语言就是现实，误以为 概念所指的就是“真实的我”。从此，我们不再相信自己原是完整的，而是不断 向外寻找“让自己更完整”的东西。 佛法里说：“迷时众生，觉时即佛。”圆满，其实从未离开过我们 。它不过 是被层层迷雾遮蔽了，我们只是暂时忘了，自己本来是谁 。找回圆满，并不是 “变得更好”，而是“成为那个原本 、真实的自己”。人生，或许正是一场从完整 中出发，在千变万化中穿越破碎，最终照见生命深处那份美好与明亮，重新归 于完整的旅程 。
viewingNote.en: The wheels roll over the dust, moving ever forward... The philosopher Deleuze once proposed: “A journey is not a matter of moving from one point to another but of a 'becoming' in which something changes in the very nature of the traveler.”At this moment, the mileage ticks upward, as if existence itself is slowly being renewed. I am not merely on the road—I am in motion, constantly becoming another “me.” The landscape of Tibet is complex and mysterious: serene lakes, deep canyons, vast pastures, primal volcanic rock fields... Sitting in the car, looking through the window, I see stern snow-capped mountains ahead. Their peaks, like countless tiny mirrors, shimmer with a cold white brilliance under the sunlight, seemingly reflecting another distant and lucid world. Dadhul was driving—young and familiar with the terrain, he avoided the usual routes, heading instead toward the most primitive and remote places. A few hours later, we stopped by a forest. Daozhang excitedly opened the trunk and began organizing his beloved photography equipment, while Dadhul took out his small chair. Soon, the ground was spread with various foods and drinks, as if we were having a “Lingka” (Tibetan: picnic) Surrounded by greenery, our bodies and minds relaxed. Dadhul, over six feet tall and dressed in traditional Tibetan clothing, stood out strikingly among us. He stood there like a tall, straight tree.I looked at him and said with a smile, “In my eyes, Tibetan friends always carry a certain light. You are romantic, wild, and beneath a simple exterior lies a delicate and tender heart. Right now, in my eyes, you seem to be shining.”Clearly surprised, his cheeks quickly flushed, and he simply smiled shyly without a word. I continued, “I have a friend named Matthew (pseudonym), a photographer for National Geographic and a member of a wildlife conservation organization. He has photographed rare birds all over the world and especially loves coming to Tibet. Once, invited by local authorities to photograph a critically endangered bird, he waited alone in the forest for seven full days.”I paused, looking into Dadhul’s eyes.“The birds finally appeared on the third day, but Matthew didn’t raise his camera. He just observed quietly and noticed they were unusually alert and anxious. He found it strange—this was their habitat, so why did they seem like intruders? A few days later, he found the flock again, and this time, through his binoculars, he spotted several people in the distance carrying bird-catching tools. In that moment, he understood.” “He immediately went down the mountain to investigate in town. Only then did he learn that these birds were highly valued, considered a ‘delicacy’ by some. Without hesitation, Matthew reported the situation, even providing the exact locations of the poachers. I once asked him, ‘They could go to jail for this. Weren’t you afraid of retaliation?’ He said, ‘No.’”I turned to Dadhul. “Do you know why he wasn’t afraid?”After a moment of silence, Dadhul spoke slowly, “My mother told me a story when I was young. A person once found a fallen Tsa-Tsa on the road and placed it back on a stone platform. Another day, someone else, seeing rain approaching, took off their shoe to cover the Tsa-Tsa to protect it from getting wet. Later, when the rain stopped, a third person passed by, saw the Tsa-tsa under the shoe, and placed it back on the platform to bask in the sun.” He paused. “The story is simple, but we Tibetans understand that form doesn’t matter; what truly matters is the intention in the moment. Matthew’s decision came from right mindfulness. He believed every life deserves respect and protection. He likely could not tolerate innocent lives being destroyed due to human greed and ignorance.”I looked at him. “Do you know why I said you were shining? Your words and Matthew’s original intention share the same gentle yet firm strength.” I walked to the car, opened a box, and took out the stone statue of the master that we hadn’t yet photographed. “We still haven’t taken pictures of this master statue. I think the most fitting time has arrived—right now, amidst the light you radiate. Simple, vivid, delicate, yet full of power.”Blushing shyly, Dadhul took the statue with both hands, gently raised it above his head, and softly chanted a sutra.“The master’s head feels very round,” Dadhul said with a smile. I nodded. “Yes, that’s how the master looked in real life. His features are strongly defined, especially his eyes—clearly and meticulously painted, with distinctwhites, pupils, and irises, giving a powerful sense of realism. His eyebrows are fine and elongated, and his lips are touched with red. The entire face feels vivid and lifelike because of its realism. It’s also due to the proportion of his features relative to his head that we immediately notice his expression—the subtle dynamism of the entire figure.” Pointing at the statue, I continued, “Look at his halo. Although thick and intricately decorated, the first thing that draws the eye is his face—that expression, like a clear spring, clean and lucid, radiating joy and faith, as if revealing a natural transparency and confidence born of settled wisdom.”Dadhul gazed at the statue, lost in thought, before saying slowly, “He looks compassionate and simple, yet there’s an energy around him I can’t quite describe.”“From the front, he appears approachable; from the side, solemn. You can sense that the artisan, when selecting the stone, was already thinking about how to best convey their understanding and vision of the master. During the carving process, the artisan seemed restrained and careful, striving to preserve the figure’s presence and aura by working with the stone.” I went on, “Look at the halo. The outer weave resembles hemp rope. The divine creatures in the Six Attributes are lively and vivid. The base is carved with a treasure vase and a group of elephants that seem straight out of a fairy tale. The entire piece exudes solemnity and spirituality amidst its complexity. The divine creatures on the Six Attributes and the elephants on the base all look like they’ve stepped out of a fairy tale, carrying an ethereal energy. The treasure vase is carved in the center of the base, flanked by intricate patterns, giving the statue a luxurious and concrete presence. The coloring, primarily in red and yellow, is both bright and subdued, highlighting the master’s nobility. Overall, it emits a solemn aura and emotional tension.” Dadhul listened intently, then suddenly said earnestly, “This statue is very much like the character of us Tibetans.”I was taken aback. “How so? He smiled. “The stone is rough and hard, but after carving, it reveals a gentle and vivid spirit. Just like us Tibetans—we may seem rugged, unrefined, and not particularly eloquent, but in our hearts, we are very soft. It’s a simplicity that is unpretentious yet detailed, unpolished yet profound.” I replied softly, “Yes, outwardly bold and free, inwardly delicate and clear. You are the same—bright and beautiful.” Now opening up, Dadhul suddenly turned serious. “Sister, did you know? We Tibetans are not afraid of death, because we believe in reincarnation.”“There was once a museum in New York focused on Tibetan culture—the Rubin Museum. They held an exhibition once, and the theme was—‘Death is not an end,’” I recalled.Dadhul nodded. “We practice, chant sutras, offer butter lamps, and circumambulate mountains. These seemingly repetitive actions are actually constant reminders to ourselves: maintain right mindfulness, be a good person. Because the world is too noisy, we need to practice repeatedly to avoid losing our way.” He paused, speaking slowly and firmly, “If a person’s intentions are good throughout their life, even after the body disappears, in the next cycle or rebirth, that goodness will guide them to a beautiful place.” I added, “In physics, this is called the ‘Law of Conservation of Energy.’ Energy cannot vanish into nothingness; it only changes form. From this perspective, we never truly leave—we merely continue to exist in another form. Death and rebirth cycle together, coexisting and interdependent.”Dadhul said playfully, “Then perhaps these statues also hold some kind of energy?”I looked at the silent master statue and smiled quietly. Yes. Every trace left by a life is quietly recorded by the universe. The body will eventually return to dust, but the spirit continues in another form—as thought, memory, will, or perhaps a sculpture...

viewingNote.zh: 第一次看到这尊黄财神造像时，我是被吸引住的。会有种直觉——这不仅 仅是一尊财神像，更像一位威严的将军，一位充满力量的护法，甚至还是一位 充满福相与富贵气息的神祇 。祂的姿态挺拔，饱满而端正，完全符合我们心 中对“财神”二字的想象和期待。因为在藏文化中黄财神，从来都不只是象征 财富的神祇那么简单，祂还是一位发了大愿、具足威德、象征护佑的护法神。 从艺术风格来看，这尊造像是一件 12-13 世纪西藏受到帕拉艺术风格影 响的一件作品 。在业内，往往一件作品如果出现了风格的融合，而且做得很出 色，人们第一反应通常是：“这可能是外来匠人做的。”因为传统上，大家会觉 得西藏本土匠人在那时候对于有文化融入的作品是不太容易处理好的。但由 于历史很难回望，很多事都无法过于绝对。所以，往往我更提倡不论是外来工 匠还是本土工匠，我们应该更多的注重作品本身的最终呈现 。 像这件作品，我觉得匠人不只是简单的“风格借用或者风格融合”，我更 愿意说，这是某位匠人通过深刻的理解 、消化之后，创造出来的属于匠人他自 己的艺术语言。这种语言既熟悉又新鲜，有继承 ，有变化，带着独属于他自己 的思考。 于时代背景而言，这件黄财神诞生在后弘期初期阿底峡入藏弘法之后，那 段文化交流频繁、艺术风格多元交织的时期 。当时帕拉艺术通过匠人、典 籍、法脉一路传入了青藏高原。随着 12 世纪中后期帕拉王朝的衰亡，西藏逐 渐成为大乘佛教、尤其是密宗的核心传承地 ，也因此迎来了一波新的文化融合 以及工艺流动。这尊黄财神，便是在这样的时代背景中诞生的。这件作品既 有帕拉艺术中结构严谨、线条流畅的优点，同时也将西藏本土的粗犷、阳刚和 那种山一般的力量感融入了其中，呈现出了一种“体积之美”。 财神发髻高高束起，头戴三叶冠，佩戴圆形耳铛，耳后后装饰有帕拉风格 常见的扇形冠结。脸型略方中带圆，双眼怒睁，但由于脸部五官的动态并没有 那么大，只是微微的，既给人一种护法的正气之感，又不会产生那种令人不安 的恐惧，反而让人感受到被保护的安心。 财神佩戴项链、璎珞、花环，下身是刻有细腻花纹的裙裤 。祂一手拿着 吐宝鼠，一手持摩尼宝珠 ，一脚踩着象征财富的圆形宝瓶 ，悠然地坐在莲台之 上 。整个坐姿既稳重又挺拔 。不论从正面或侧面看，形体饱满、宽厚 、结 实，且头、胸、手臂、腿部 、腰部等肢体都能看到明确区分与联系，转折自 然，形面之间起伏得当，不突兀 、不混乱，没有松散或空洞的感觉 。在整个坐 姿中，肚子的部分也很突出，圆圆的形状像球体一样饱满，轻轻地落在大腿 上，那种“福气满满”的视觉印象，是非常典型的黄财神形象特征，同样也使 得作品，给人“座得住、压得稳”的感觉 。这种由不动如山的气质所带给我们 的体积之美的构成，是经过匠人的反复观察和构造而来，才会使得作品既符 合这个题材想说的话，又具有审美的节奏与秩序。 同样，莲台也值得一提 。莲台是帕拉风格中常见的大型宽叶莲瓣设计，莲 瓣排列整齐、大气 、节奏明快 。上下缀满了连珠纹，一圈大、一圈小，层层递 进，整个莲台像一个层层递进的梯形结构，于视觉和结构上都增强了稳定感和 庄严感 。 王朝闻老师曾强调，“雕塑的体积感和量感，是一种体现雕塑实体性与审 美逻辑的内在因素，而非尺寸上的表面张扬。” 而这件财神造像就非常的符合 这句话，虽然尺寸不大，但在视觉心理上，且并不亚于一块大石头般的厚 重、沉稳 。匠人对实体存在的理解 、对形体结构的把握、对空间节奏的感 知，以及对形式与精神统一的追求达成了统一，很是难得。
viewingNote.en: I was captivated with this statue of yellow Jambhala from first moment I saw it. A clear intuition arose that this was far more than a simple wealth deity. Here was the commanding presence of a majestic general, the formidable energy of a powerful dharmapala, and the abundant aura of a divinity overflowing with fortune and nobility. His posture—erect, robust, and dignified—perfectly embodies our deepest imaginings of a "wealth god." And rightly so, for in Tibetan culture, Yellow Jambhala is never merely a symbol of prosperity; he is a protector, a guardian who has taken profound vows, embodying both majestic virtue and benevolent safeguarding. From an artistic style perspective, this statue is a work from 12th-13th century Tibet, influenced by the Pala artistic style. In the industry, when a piece exhibits stylistic fusion and is executed excellently, the first reaction is often: "This was probably made by a foreign artisan." Traditionally, it was believed that local Tibetan artisans of that time struggled to handle works involving cultural integration well. However, since history is hard to look back upon, many things cannot be stated too absolutely. Therefore, I often advocate that, whether by foreign or local artisans, we should focus more on the final presentation of the work itself. In the case of this statue, I believe the artisan did not simply "borrow or blend styles"; I would rather say that this is an artisan, through deep understanding and digestion, creating his own artistic language. This language is both familiar and fresh, incorporating inheritance and change, carrying thoughts unique to himself. In terms of historical context, this Yellow Jambhala was born during the early period of the Later Diffusion of Buddhism, after Atisha came to Tibet to propagate the teachings—a time of frequent cultural exchanges and interwoven artistic styles. Pala art was transmitted to the Tibetan Plateau through artisans, scriptures, and religious lineages. With the decline of the Pala Dynasty in the mid-to-late 12th century, Tibet gradually became the core inheritor of Mahayana Buddhism, especially Vajrayana, and thus welcomed a new wave of cultural integration and craftsmanship flow. This Yellow Jambhala was born in such an era. The work possesses the strengths of Pala art—rigorous structure and smooth lines—while also integrating the raw boldness, masculinity, and mountain-like strength of native Tibet, presenting a "beauty of volume." The deity's hair is tied high in a topknot, adorned with a three-leaf crown, wearing circular earrings, and behind the ears is a fan-shaped crown knot typical of the Pala style. The face is slightly square yet round, with eyes wide open in a wrathful gaze. However, the dynamism of the facial features is not overly exaggerated—just subtle—giving a sense of the dharmapala's righteous aura without evoking unsettling fear, but rather a feeling of protective reassurance. Adorned with a necklace, garlands, and a floral wreath, the deity’s lower body is draped in finely detailed dhoti trousers. In one hand, he grips a Nehulay—a treasure-emitting mongoose—and in the other, a luminous wish-fulfilling jewel. One foot rests firmly upon a round vase, emblematic of wealth, as he sits in serene composure upon a lotus throne. His seated posture radiates stability and upright dignity. Viewed from any angle, the figure exhibits a robust, voluminous presence: broad-shouldered, solidly built, with clearly defined yet harmoniously connected limbs—head, chest, arms, legs, and waist. Each transition between forms feels organic and intentional, with no abrupt shifts or unresolved voids. The belly, prominently rounded and full like a globe, rests gently upon the thighs—a classic feature of Yellow Jambhala iconography that conveys both abundant fortune and grounded physicality. This sense of weight and unshakable presence, reminiscent of a mountain, results from the artisan’s meticulous study and mastery of volume. The work transcends mere representation, achieving a balance of symbolic intent and sculptural grace—a testament to both devotional insight and aesthetic order. The lotus throne itself is a remarkable feature. Executed in the classic Pala manner, it is composed of large, broad lotus petals arranged with a grand and rhythmic precision. Above and below, rows of pearl motifs—alternating in size—create a layered, progressive effect. The entire throne takes on a tiered, trapezoidal form, a design that masterfully reinforces both the visual solemnity and structural stability of the sculpture. Teacher Wang Zhaoweng once emphasized, "The sense of volume and mass in sculpture is an internal factor reflecting the substantiality and aesthetic logic of the sculptural entity, not a superficial flamboyance of size." This Jambhala statue fits this description very well. Though small in size, its visual and psychological impact is no less than the heaviness and steadiness of a large rock. The artisan's understanding of physical presence, grasp of structural form, perception of spatial rhythm, and pursuit of the unity of form and spirit are all unified—a rare achievement.

viewingNote.zh: 这尊融合了尼泊尔艺术风格的金刚萨垛造像，带有典型的夏鲁寺艺术特征 。 一听到“金刚”两个字，我们第一反应往往会联想到勇猛、怒目等具有极强张力 的画面。但这尊造像给我们的视觉感受却恰然相反，祂是“忿怒中的温柔者”。 金刚萨埵这个题材，是藏传佛教中非常重要的本尊之一。“金刚”象征着 不变、不坏 、真实、本觉和空性；“萨埵”则意为友情、心者、勇士。因此，金 刚萨埵这个名字在文化中可直译为：具有金刚之心的觉者，或者是有勇气直面 与转化烦恼的勇士。 在密教传统中，金刚总持是佛陀究竟的法身，是智慧与空性的圆满体现 。 然而，法身超越时空，抽象而无相，不易被众生直接感知和依止 。于是，金刚 总持以种种化身示现，回应不同众生的需要。金刚萨埵便是其中极为关键的一 种化身——专为帮助修行者消除业障 、恢复清净而出现 。 佛教中“化身”的观念，揭示了一个重要的哲学关系：真理若不化为可触、可 用的形式，就无法进入人的生命。金刚总持象征“究竟”，而金刚萨埵则是这种 “究竟”在修行路途中的“实践形式”。换言之：金刚总持是太阳本身；金刚萨 埵是穿过云层，能够温暖人心的光 。 人类的生命不是在无尘的空境里展开，而是在业力、习气和情绪的漩涡中 不断轮转。即使知道有智慧与空性的终极真理，如果心灵没有经过清理与净 化，我们也无法真正承载它。金刚萨埵的意义正在于此：祂不是终点，而是帮 助修行者“抵达终点”的关键力量。通过祂的修持，修行人不断消解业障，使 内心逐渐透明，从而使智慧得以在心中显现 。 在现代语境中，祂代表了人类必须直面并清理内心阴影的过程 。我们每个 人心中都有过往的创伤、未消解的执念与负面循环，若不加以面对，就会形成 “心理业障”，阻碍我们与真实的自我接触 。就像真理从来不是一蹴而就的，而 是通过“去蔽”的过程逐渐显现 。金刚萨埵正是这个“去蔽”的化身，把隐藏 在本体中的光带回到日常生命。 当我第一次看到这尊金刚萨垛造像时，也许是因为融入了尼泊尔风格的原 因，带给我一种极强的“结实”感 。造像从肩膀到膝盖的转折处，如方形的折 来源：Christie’s,New York,Friday,20 March,2009, no.1365 角般有力；肌肉结构臌胀，以方形的块面呈现；肩膀偏方正，肢体厚重，下面 底座稳固大气，整体看上去都是有一种方形的状态 。这种整体的“方形语 言”，给人一种坚实、强壮、有支撑力的力量感 。 尽管形态结实，但与此同时，祂又带着一股仙气 。略微偏转的头部姿 态，手持金刚杵与铃的动势，为端庄的坐姿增添了动感 。面庞圆润，额头饱 满，姿态挺拔，目光略垂的眼部形状秀气 ，且刻线柔和、飘逸，神情间流露出 温和、清朗的“神性”。加上嘴角微扬的笑意所折射出的情绪，让人能感受到祂 内在的清净与喜悦 。 金刚萨垛头戴五叶宝冠、脸型圆中带方、额髻宽广、眼 型像细长的杏 仁、鼻部小巧高挺，这种开脸特征为夏鲁寺造像的典型风格。宽大的耳垂上佩 戴着圆形耳环，身上所戴的臂钏、璎珞和裙边，都以颗颗饱满的连珠纹装 饰；在繁与简之间恰到好处地取得了平衡。底座上的莲瓣清晰明朗、舒展有 度，在底层大颗连珠纹的托举中，有一种呼吸般的张合节奏，使得整尊造像在 自然吐纳之间透着一种大气和高贵。 尽管造像整体呈现的是比较壮硕的感觉，但两侧脑后轻薄的飘带材质，恰 如其分地增添了一种“风”的存在 。这种“轻”与“重”的对比，也让整尊造像 更具灵性与动感了。飘带在脑后舒展，又像是背光的晕环，为整体营造出了一种 更为具象的神圣场域 。凝望间，我仿佛看到祂像是飘在空中，正乘坐一片祥云 飘向而来，从空性中显现，如祂名字所象征的那般纯净、和煦 。 祂就像一道内在觉性的光，悄然降临 。祂不为让你逃避罪与苦，而是提醒 你将它带入光中，那像是一种唤醒——你或许曾迷失、堕落 、错失方向，但你 从未真正失去清净的本性 。如果你愿意承认、转身，那这份勇气将化为作金刚 般真实、坚不可摧的力量 。因为，清净从来不是因修行而得来，而是你本自具 足，你只需要去拨开那些遮蔽它的层层迷雾罢了。
viewingNote.en: This statue of Vajrasattva, blending Nepalese artistic styles, bears the distinctive characteristics of Shalu Monastery art. Usually, at the mention of "Vajra" (diamond or thunderbolt), our first instinct might be to associate it with fierce, wrathful imagery brimming with intensity. Yet this statue is quite the opposite. It is a "gentle presence amidst wrath." Vajrasattva is one of the most important deities in Tibetan Buddhism. "Vajra" symbolizes immutability, indestructibility, truth, innate awareness, and emptiness; "sattva" means friendship, the mind, or a warrior. Thus, the name Vajrasattva can be directly translated in cultural context as "the awakened one with a vajra heart" or "the warrior courageous enough to face and transform afflictions." In Vajrayana tradition, Vajradhara (Dorje Chang) is the ultimate dharmakaya (truth body) of the Buddha, the perfect embodiment of wisdom and emptiness. However, the dharmakaya transcends time and space, is abstract and formless, and is not easily perceived or relied upon directly by sentient beings. Thus, Vajradhara manifests in various forms to respond to the needs of different beings. Vajrasattva is one of these crucial manifestations—specifically appearing to help practitioners purify karmic obstacles and restore inner purity. The concept of "manifestation" in Buddhism reveals an important philosophical relationship: if truth does not take a tangible, usable form, it cannot enter human life. Vajradhara symbolizes the "ultimate," while Vajrasattva is the "practical form" of this ultimate on the path of practice. In other words, Vajradhara is the sun itself; Vajrasattva is the sunlight that pierces through the clouds to warm the human heart. Human life does not unfold in a dust-free void but continuously revolves through vortices of karma, habits, and emotions. Even knowing the ultimate truth of wisdom and emptiness, if the mind has not been cleansed and purified, we cannot truly embody it. This is precisely the significance of Vajrasattva: not as the endpoint, but as the key force that helps practitioners "reach the endpoint." Through practices dedicated to Vajrasattva, practitioners continually dissolve karmic obstacles, gradually making the mind transparent so that wisdom can manifest within. In contemporary terms, Vajrasattva symbolizes the journey of facing and purifying our inner darkness. Within each of us lie past wounds, unresolved attachments, and harmful patterns. If left unaddressed, these become “karmic mental barriers” that hinder our connection with the true self. Just as truth is never revealed in an instant, but gradually emerges through a process of “unveiling,” Vajrasattva embodies this very act of “unveiling”—bringing the light hidden within our essence back into everyday life. Shalu Monastery,Xizang 13-14th Century Gilded copper Size: 17.6cm Provenance: Christie’s, New York, Friday, March 20, 2009, no. 1365 VAJRASATTVA When I first saw this statue of Vajrasattva, perhaps due to its incorporation of Nepalese styles, it conveyed a strong sense of "solidity." The transitions from the shoulders to the knees are powerful, like squared angles; the muscular structure is robust, presented in block-like forms; the shoulders are broad and square, the limbs substantial, and the base is stable and grand—the overall impression is one of squared solidity. This "language of squares" evokes a sense of firmness, strength, and supportive power. Despite its solid form, the statue also carries an ethereal grace. The slightly turned head and the dynamic posture of holding the vajra and bell add movement to the dignified seated position. The face is full and rounded, the forehead broad, the posture upright, and the eyes—gently downcast—are elegantly shaped with soft, flowing lines, expressing a gentle and clear "divinity." The subtle smile at the corners of the mouth reflects an inner purity and joy. Vajrasattva wears a five-leaf crown, with a face that is round yet slightly squared, a broad forehead, almond-shaped eyes, and a small, high-bridged nose—facial features typical of Shalu Monastery sculptures. The large earlobes are adorned with circular earrings, and the armbands, necklaces, and skirt are decorated with rows of full, bead-like motifs, striking a perfect balance between complexity and simplicity. The lotus petals on the base are clear, well-defined, and gracefully spread, supported by large beads at the bottom, creating a rhythmic sense of expansion and contraction, as if breathing. This gives the entire statue a noble and majestic presence, as if naturally inhaling and exhaling. Although the overall impression of the statue is robust, the thin, flowing ribbons on either side of the head aptly suggest the presence of "wind." This contrast between "lightness" and "weight" adds spirituality and dynamism to the sculpture. The ribbons flowing behind the head resemble a halo of light, creating a more concrete sacred space around the figure. Gazing at it, I almost feel as if it is floating in the air, riding on a cloud of auspiciousness, emerging from emptiness— pure and gentle, just as its name symbolizes. It is like a light of inner awareness, quietly descending. It does not ask you to escape sin or suffering but reminds you to bring them into the light—a kind of awakening: you may have been lost, fallen, or strayed, but you have never truly lost your innate purity. If you are willing to acknowledge and turn around, this courage will transform into a power as real and unshakable as a vajra. For purity is never something gained through practice; it is inherently yours. You need only clear away the layers of mist that obscure it.

viewingNote.zh: 佛塔，是印度梵文 Stupa（窣堵波）的音译，也称为浮屠。佛塔作为佛教建 筑的代表，不仅是佛教信仰和文化的重要象征，还承载着丰富的历史、宗教、艺 术和文化意义。佛塔最早起源于释迦牟尼圆寂后，弟子们为了纪念佛陀，而用石 块建塔以埋葬其骨灰。最初的佛塔多为简单的土堆或石堆，用以纪念。随着佛 教的兴盛，佛塔逐渐演变成了复杂的石造建筑，成为了佛教信仰的重要象征。在 印度，阿育王统治时期，佛教被列为国教，佛塔的建立达到了空前的高涨，分建 了八万四千座佛塔，这一时期的佛塔多为覆钵式塔。随着佛教的传播发展和在亚 洲各地的兴起，佛塔历经漫长的历史演变过程后，形成了多种的风格和形式。佛 塔被视为佛陀圣物的安放处，象征着佛陀的身体、言语和心灵。它们也通常建造 和出现在具有宗教意义的地点，作为供奉、祈祷和冥想的中心。 这座九到十世纪的尼泊尔理查维晚期佛塔，为红铜鎏金铸造，它像是一座缩 小的庙宇建筑，在微缩世界中讲述着宏达叙事。佛塔的顶部采用了典型的螺旋形 设计，这种形式在佛教建筑中被称为“相轮”，象征着宇宙的中心与佛陀的智慧之 光。圆形的半球形顶盖，称为“覆钵”，代表了宇宙的无边无际，以及佛教中解脱 轮回的理想。覆钵的形式为早期佛塔形制的延续，同样，整个上半部分也 为尼泊尔常见的早期佛塔形制。衔接着覆钵的覆式莲瓣宽大、舒展，瓣尖微微上 翘，此种莲瓣风格延续了印度那烂陀七到八世纪的早期风格。 佛塔的中段和底座则是多层的方形结构，这些结构代表了佛教宇宙观中的不 同世界。整个塔看上去就像一座完整的缩小版庙宇，塔的四周等分布局，对称的 四扇门楣前分别供奉着四尊佛，因为年代久远已经遗失。平衡对称的布局是宇宙 秩序的镜像，更是对和谐、平衡理念的诠释。在这种对称中，我们仿佛看到了天 地万物间的相互依存与制约。每一面都如同宇宙的一扇窗，具有空间的透视 性，透过它，我们可以窥见不同的风景，却又在根本上保持着一致与统一。塔身 的门廊上装饰有对称的八根立柱，柱体表面的切面塑造写实，装饰有连珠纹和部 分几何形图案。部分立柱从侧面看过去为镂空铸造，体现了当时尼泊尔精湛的铸 造工艺。立柱的上方分别有两只面对面站立的摩羯，摩羯长鼻、利齿，翻卷的尾 翼似能翻江倒海。在古印度神话中，摩羯原本是性恶的海中巨兽，会加害于人。 然而，在佛教的度化后，摩羯的形象像神兽般灵动、温顺且可爱。 随着视线顺延向上，出现在门楣中间的为天福之面。天福之面，源于上古时期的印度，是湿婆神用第三只眼变出的一只面目狰狞、双眼外凸、嘴巴巨大且极 为贪食的怪兽。然而，由于其忠诚，最终被封为专司门户的门神，其兽面常出现 在佛塔、寺庙等宗教建筑物的门楣中央或拱道之上，位置高于其他神祇。天福之 面的塑造具有极强的视觉冲击力和表现力。其面目狰狞、凶猛异常，表现出一种 震慑人心的力量感。它作为门神的象征，守护着佛教的神圣空间，同时也传达着 一种威严和庄重的感觉。在塔基的上层其中一面铸造有心形莲瓣，起到了标注塔 方位的作用，塔基上的此种莲瓣与十世纪印度库尔奇哈尔风格造像的莲瓣如出一 辙。塔基莲台的其他三个面，以折叠的塑造方式层层扩大，如同生命的阶 梯。整个佛塔上中下的结构设计，在圆形与方形两种形状的巧妙结合中，不仅展 现了建筑形式上的变化与丰富，更带有着无限的哲学关系。 试想，如果我们真的能够将自己缩小，当站在这座佛塔面前，那将是一种何 等的体验？那一刻，所有的喧嚣与纷扰都将远去，只留下内心的平静与敬畏。我 们会惊讶于这座微缩庙宇所展现出的对称、均衡与和谐之美，那是一种超越了物 质世界的视觉震撼。在这座雕塑构建的空间里，我们会不由自主地放慢脚步，让 心灵在这份宁静与美好中得到真正的休憩与升华。 这座别致、特殊的佛塔曾经可能供奉在一座重要的寺庙中，承载着信徒们的 信仰和虔诚，见证着无数次的宗教仪式和祈祷活动。尽管经历了千年的传世，这 座佛塔保存完好，这得是无数人代人的接力传承。相较同时期或者更早的类似佛 塔而言，这件佛塔是目前见到的唯一一件几乎完整的作品，大多数都是相轮部分 破损或遗失，不论稀缺性、重要性、艺术性角度而言，这件九到十世纪的尼泊尔 佛塔都是一件不可多得的艺术珍品。
viewingNote.en: Pagoda, another term for the Sanskrit word "Stupa" literally means “heap” or “pile”. As a prominent form of Buddhist architecture, pagodas serve as important symbols of Buddhist faith and culture, embodying rich historical, religious, artistic, and cultural significance. The origin of Buddhist pagodas can be traced back to the period following the death of Shakyamuni (the historical Buddha), when his disciples built mounds of earth or stone to enshrine his ashes in remembrance of him. These early pagodas were simple structures used primarily for commemoration. As Buddhism flourished, these structures evolved into more complex stone buildings, becoming a significant symbol of the Buddhist faith. During the reign of King Ashoka in India, Buddhism was declared the state religion, leading to a surge in the construction of pagodas, with 84,000 built under his directive. Most pagodas from this period were bowl-shaped, symbolizing the simplicity and universality of the Buddhist teachings. As Buddhism spread across Asia, pagodas underwent a long process of evolution, developing into various styles and forms. The pagoda is regarded as a place for enshrining sacred Buddhist relics, symbolizing the Buddha's body, speech, and mind. These structures are often located in places of religious importance, serving as centers for worship, prayer, and meditation. This particular pagoda statue, dating from the 9th to 10th century during the late Licchavi dynasty of Nepal, is made of red copper gilt and resembles a miniature temple and appears to be telling a grand story in its small form. The top of the pagoda features a typical spiral design, known in Buddhist architecture as the "Dharma wheel," which symbolizes the center of the universe and the radiance of the Buddha's wisdom. The circular hemispherical top, called the "Bumpa," represents the boundless universe and the Buddhist ideal of liberation from the cycle of reincarnation. The form of the Bumpa is a continuation of the early pagoda shape (Figure 1), with the entire upper part reflecting common early pagoda styles found in Nepal. The lotus petals connected to the Bumpa are broad and slightly upturned, continuing the early style of Nalanda, India, from the 7th to 8th centuries (Figure 2). The middle section and base of the pagoda consist of multi-layered square structures, representing different realms in Buddhist cosmology. The entire tower resembles a scaled-down version of a temple, with four symmetrical door lintels arranged evenly around the structure. These once housed four Buddha statues, which have since been lost to time. This balanced and symmetrical layout reflects the order of the universe and embodies concepts of harmony and balance. Within this symmetry, we perceive the interdependence of all things in the world. Each side acts as a window intothe universe, offering varied perspectives while maintaining a fundamental unity.The pagoda's porch is adorned with eight symmetrical columns, realistically shaped with bead and geometric patterns. Some columns are hollow-cast when viewed from the side, showcasing the exquisite casting craftsmanship of Nepal at the time. Above the columns, two Makaras face each other. In ancient Indian mythology, Makaras were malevolent sea creatures. However, through the influence of Buddhism, the Makara was transformed into a symbol of a divine, gentle beast.As we look upward, the Kirtimukha appears in the center of the lintel. Originating from ancient India, the Kirtimukha is a monstrous creature created by the god Shiva using his third eye. It has a fierce appearance with protruding eyes and a large mouth, symbolizing its insatiable hunger. Due to its loyalty, the Kirtimukha was eventually appointed as the guardian of sacred spaces, and its image is often found on the lintels or arches of religious buildings, positioned higher than other deities. The depiction of the Kirtimukha has a strong visual impact and expressiveness, conveying a sense of majesty and solemnity. It serves as a symbol of the door gods, guarding the sacred space of Buddhism. On one side of the upper layer of the tower base, a heart-shaped lotus petal indicates the orientation of the pagoda. This lotus petal design matches that of a 10th-century Indian Kurkihar-style statue (Figure 3). The other three sides of the pagoda base expand layer by layer in a stepped pattern, resembling a ladder of life. The pagoda's structural design, with its clever combination of circular and square shapes, not only showcases the richness and variety of architectural forms but also embodies profound philosophical concepts. Imagine if we could really shrink ourselves and stand in front of this pagoda, what kind of experience would it be? At that moment, all the hustle and bustle will fade away, leaving only inner peace and reverence. We will be amazed by the symmetry, balance, and harmony displayed by this miniature temple, which is a visual shock that transcends the material world. In the space constructed by this sculpture, we will involuntarily slow down our pace, allowing our souls to truly rest and sublimate in this tranquility and beauty. This unique pagoda may have been enshrined in an important temple, carrying the faith and devotion of its followers and witnessing countless religious ceremonies and prayers. Despite being over a thousand years old, this pagoda remains remarkably well-preserved, having been passed down through generations. Compared to similar pagodas from the same period or earlier, this piece is currently the only one in nearly perfect condition; most others have lost their upper sections. From the perspectives of rarity, historical significance, and artistic value, this Nepalese pagoda from the 9th to 10th century is a rare artistic treasure.

viewingNote.zh: 五世达赖喇嘛阿旺罗桑嘉措（1617-1682），是藏传佛教格鲁派历史上具有重要地位的一位宗教领袖 。 1642 年，在蒙古和硕特部固始汗的帮助下，他和四世班禅一起建立了格鲁派在藏区的宗教统治权 。1644 年满族政权入关之后，他不断加强与清廷的联系，顺治九年（1652）更亲往北京，进献千件贡礼 。一年 后，顺治帝赐金册金印，封为“西天大善自在佛所领天下释教普通瓦赤喇怛喇达赖喇嘛”称号，自此 ，达赖 喇嘛在蒙藏地区的宗教领袖地位正式得到确认 ，格鲁派的发展进入鼎盛时期 。 此尊造像尺寸虽小却写实生动，尊者颧骨高隆 ，下颏尖凸，呈鹅蛋脸型 ，神情坚毅 ，青春的面庞里充 盈着智者的慈和与睿智，端坐于双层卡垫之上，躯体端正挺直 ，内着僧坎和僧裙，外披袈裟和僧氅，圆鼓 壮硕的肢体在膨胀的袈裟的每个细节上得以体现；袈裟华丽贴体，衣褶处理流畅自然 ，雕刻甚为精美 ，花 纹繁而不乱，錾刻力道深刻均匀，尤能体现工匠在虔诚的宗教感情激发下的深厚功力，以及供养人对尊者 无比崇敬的心态 。底座造型软垫堆栈，别致少见；锦垫錾刻繁密卷草纹及莲花，刀法流畅细腻 ，台座底部 是完好如初的封底 。卡垫背面整齐錾刻两行行藏文铭文 ，汉译为“胜利的王，遍知一切的阿旺 · 罗桑嘉 措，贤德，我们尊敬他 。”为此像的身份提供了可靠的信息 。 五世达赖喇嘛的造像坊间流传颇广，但这是目前已知唯一一尊纯金铸造 ，且明确指出其身份的，其宗 教意义无与伦比 。不仅尺寸小巧，同时艺术手法自然写实，整体雕刻工艺极为精湛，可称为西藏祖师类造 像的巅峰之作 。
viewingNote.en: Ngagwang Lobzang Gyatso (1617-1682), the Great Fifth Dalai Lama, was a significant religious leader in the history of the Gelug Sect of Tibetan Buddhism. In 1642, with the assistance of the Mongol Güshi Khan of the Khoshut tribe, he established the religious dominance of the Gelug sect in the Tibetan region, alongside the Fourth Panchen Lama. In 1644, following the rise of the Qing Dynasty, he strengthened his ties with the Qing court. In the ninth year of the Shunzhi Emperor’s reign (1652), he even traveled to Beijing to present a thousand tributary offerings. A year later, the Shunzhi Emperor bestowed upon him a royal appointment decree made of gold leaf and a golden seal, conferring upon him the title Earth Under the Great Benevolent Self-subsisting Buddha of the Western Paradise “Overseer of the Buddhist Faith on ”. From then on, the Fifth Dalai Lama Gelug Sect experienced a period of prosperity and development. ’s status as a religious leader in the Mongol and Tibetan regions was officially recognized, and the This small sculpture is remarkably lifelike despite its size. The Great Fifth has prominent cheekbones and a pointed chin, exuding a resolute, compassionate, and wise expression. He sits upright on a double-layered cushion with straight posture. He wears an inner monastic waistcoat and robe, with an outer kasaya and monk’s cloak draped over. His body appears robust, emphasized by the billowing folds of his kasaya, and the overall presentation achieves perfect, harmonious proportions. The highlight of this sculpture is the intricate detailing of the kasaya clothing folds at the elbows and legs shapes the sculpture ’s folds. It combines eastern line techniques with western realism. The overlapping of ’s flat relationships. The drapery gracefully follows the contours of the figure, creating a harmonious relationship between the folds. The width, layout, propor tions, and intricacy of the kasaya’s folds vary from top to bottom, left to right, with a perfect blend of horizontal - and vertical elements, complexity and simplicity. The base of the sculpture is a cushion throne rather than a lotus seat, a unique and uncommon design re sembling two well-arranged overlapping scripture books. The bottom of the pedestal remains intact. On - the back of the cushion, there are neatly inscribed two lines of Tibetan script, which translates to Victory, the Omniscient Ngagwang Lobzang Gyatso, the Great, We Show Great Reverence for Him “The King of ”. This provides reliable information about the identity of the figure. While sculptures of the Fifth Dalai Lama are quite common, this is the only known sculpture cast in pure gold that specifically identifies him. Its religious significance is unparalleled. In its small size, the artistry is exceptionally naturalistic, and the overall sculpting craftsmanship is superb, which can be considered the pinnacle of Tibetan patriarch figure sculpture.

viewingNote.zh: 8 世纪中叶，瞿波罗王建立了帕拉王朝 。领土覆盖今天印度东部的 比哈尔邦 、西孟加拉邦以及孟加拉国 。帕拉王朝历经四百多年之久 ，历 代君王都鼎力支持佛教及佛教艺术发展 ，是印度佛教达到最后的顶峰 。 菩提迦耶的大菩提寺，那烂陀的大僧院 ，在此时发展迅速 。帕拉王室 还建立了飞行寺、超戒寺等大寺院 ，大乘佛教与密教共同发展 。在这些 寺院和僧院之中，除了印度人 ，也有来自亚洲各地的朝圣僧侣 、留学僧 和佛教信徒 ，可谓盛极一时。 此尊嵌银嵌红铜的世尊观音正是印度帕拉王朝 11 世纪造像艺术中 的绝佳典范 。观世音菩萨有三十三种化身，世尊观世音是三十三种化身 之一 。观音的整体气韵和细节刻画均彰显着帕拉造像的独特美感，合 金青铜的材质在累世传承中变得愈发细润柔和，映射出观音圣洁的光 辉，昭示这是一件不可多得的精品 。具体来看，观音头戴小三叶冠，帽 冠边沿嵌银且用连珠纹装饰，突出层次感；发髻扁平高耸，发辫盘束宛 如麻绳 ，一股股层次分明 。观音发髻前化现阿弥陀佛一尊，点明观音身 份及莲部归属；耳上扇形冠结横出，缯带婉转上扬，夸张而富有装饰 性 。观音面庞塑造立体生动，额间有长方形白毫，双目炯炯有神，眉弓 高挑 ，末端与鼻梁相连 ，嘴唇丰满，向中间收紧，为帕拉王朝造像典型 开脸 ，极具南亚人种特点；嵌银的双眼、嵌红铜的嘴唇与近黄铜质地的 古铜色肌肤相互对比下，遂形成了写实的逼真效果 。观音双肩宽阔，胸 脯厚实，双臂圆润，体态刻画自然写实，菩萨健硕的胸肌及柔软的小腹 均采用打磨的方式呈现出其轮廓线条，体现出印度古典主义雕塑技艺对 人物体态的精准塑造 。胸前佩戴嵌银单层项链 ，下方为嵌宝石璎珞，双 臂饰嵌宝石菱形臂钏；胸前巾帛嵌红铜体现边缘线再加阴线刻画，圣 索整体嵌银自左肩自然跨下。观音下着贴身裙裤，腰带嵌以红铜，工匠 采用阴线的方式来表现布料的衣纹 及贴身的质感，裙裤嵌以红铜收 尾，双腿自然摆放 ，腿部肌肉线条塑造圆润饱满 。左手执莲花，右手轻 垂于膝盖施予愿印，体现出观音留驻世间救度苍生的伟大发誓愿，手脚 掌心皆镶嵌银质的宝相花，装饰华丽，用料不惜工本；两侧的枝干蜿蜒 生长 ，绽放于肩头，在严谨细腻中见飘逸之美 ，富丽堂皇的同时又富有 生机和活力 。观音身下为仰覆式莲座 ，上下缘装饰连珠纹 ，颗粒饱满，排列规 整；上方连珠 纹稍小，底部连珠 纹 则极 为夸张 ，仿佛一粒粒镶嵌上 去，珠圆玉润 、严丝合缝 ，极富视觉冲击力的效果 。莲瓣满饰一周，平 整而舒展 ，上下错落有致；在莲瓣与莲台的空隙 之间镶嵌有三圈红 铜；莲台背面的莲瓣上刻有两行古梵文 1 ，字体娟秀，笔画刚劲有力，排 列十分规整 。 此尊世尊观音体现了典型帕拉成熟时期的艺术风格，菩萨挺拔的躯 体恰到好处的形成了坚定英勇的力量感；如弓的双眉 、上扬的嘴角和微 含的下颌正悲悯的注视众生；如山高耸的发髻旁边的缯带仿佛在随风 向上飘动，给寂静相的菩萨平添了威严肃杀之气；娇嫩的花茎和鲜艳的 蓓蕾化为菩萨无边之爱向众生飘洒甘露；每个部分的塑造各有其高妙 之处 ，散发出一种异乎寻常的寂静力量 。
viewingNote.en: n the mid-8th century, GopalaⅠestablished the Pala Dynasty. The territory covered present-day Indian states of Bihar, West Bengal, and Bangladesh. The Pala Dynasty lasted for over four hundred years, and successive rulers strongly supported the devel￾opment of Buddhism and Buddhist art, marking the pinnacle of Indian Buddhist civili￾zation. The Mahabodhi Temple in Bodh Gaya and Nalanda, the renowned mahavihara (Buddhist monastic university), flourished during this period. The Pala royals also founded major monasteries such as the Odantapuri and the Vikramashila, fostering the growth of Mahayana Buddhism and Esoteric Buddhism side by side. Within these tem￾ples and monasteries, besides Indians, there were also pilgrim monks, international monks, and Buddhist devotees from various parts of Asia, leading to a time of great prosperity. This silver and red copper-inlaid sculpture of the Sacred Guanyin is a splendid exam ple of the 11th-century sculptural art of the Pala Dynasty in India. The Sacred one is - one of the thirty-three manifestations of Guanyin. The overall grace and detailed depic￾tion highlight the unique aesthetic of Pala sculpting. The material (mainly copper alloy) has grown increasingly refined and gentle through generations’ inheritance, reflecting the sanctified radiance of Guanyin and signifying this as an exceptionally rare master piece. In specific details, the Sacred Guanyin wears a small tripartite crown on the head. - The edge of the crown is silver-inlaid and adorned with a beaded pattern, creating a sense of dimension. The hair topknot is flat and rises high, with braids coiled like hemp ropes, forming distinct layers. In front of the hair topknot, there is an image of Amitabha Buddha, indicating the Guanyin’s identity as a member of Padma (Lotus) family. The fan-shaped crown knots on the ears extend horizontally, and the ribbons elegantly rise with exaggerated style. The facial features are dynamically sculpted, with a square white urna on the forehead. The eyes are bright and piercing, while the arched eyebrows are elegantly raised, extending to connect with the bridge of the nose. The lips are full and taper towards the center, representing a typical face carving style in Pala Dynasty sculpt ing and embodying distinctive South Asian characteristics. The silver-inlaid eyes, cop - - per-inlaid lips, and the bronze-colored skin, which looks like brass material, create a contrast that results in a realistic and lifelike effect. The shoulders are broad, the chest is solid, and the arms are rounded. The depiction of the body is naturally realistic, pre senting the robust pectoral muscles and supple abdomen using a polishing method, - showcasing the precision of Indian classical sculpting in portraying the human form. The chest is adorned with a silver-inlaid single-layer necklace, with a gem-studded fes￾toon hanging below. The arms are embellished with diamond-shaped gemstone arm lets. The chest cloth is inlaid with red copper, accentuating the edge lines and engraving - with incised lines, and the sacred cord (sash) is entirely silver-inlaid, naturally draping from the left shoulder to below the waist. The lower part of the figure is dressed in close-fitting trousers with a red copper belt. Artisan have employed engraved lines to depict the fabric’s texture and the snug feel of the clothing. The end of trousers is inlaid 比例 Scale 1:1 Pala Dynasty ｜11-12th century ｜Copper alloy Silver Red copper ｜H: 12.5cm Sacred Guanyin 25 with red copper, and the legs are naturally positioned, with muscular contours sculpted to appear full and rounded. In the left hand, the Sacred Guanyin holds a lotus flower, while the right hand rests lightly on the knee, forming wish-granting gesture. This gesture reflects the Guanyin’s great vow to remain in the world to save sentient beings. Both the palms and the soles of the feet are inlaid with silver mixed flowers pattern, adorned splendidly at all costs. Branches gracefully wind and bloom over the shoulders, exhibiting a sense of elegance within meticulous precision, combining grandeur with vitality and liveliness. Under the Guanyin, there is an inverted lotus pedestal. Both the upper and lower edges are decorated with a beaded pattern, full of rounded beads arranged neatly. The bead pattern at the top is smaller, while the one at the bottom is relatively exaggerated, resembling individually inlaid beads. They are round, lustrous, and join tightly, creating great visual appeal. The lotus petals are fully adorned around the pedestal, smooth and gracefully extended with a well-arranged effect. Three concentric rings of red copper are inlaid between the lotus petals and the lotus pedestal’s gaps. On the back of the lotus pedestal’s petals, two lines of ancient Sanskrit1 are engraved. The script with powerful strokes is elegant and arranged in a very orderly fashion. This sculpture embodies the typical art of mature style in the Pala dynasty. The yin’s upright body forms a perfect sence of steadfast and courageous strength. The Guanarched eyebrows, upturned edges of the mouth, and the gently inward inclined chin all convey a compassionate gaze towards sentient beings. The ribbons next to the towering topknot seem to flutter upwards with the wind, adding an aura of majesty to the serene sculpture. Delicate flower stems and vibrant buds transform into the boundless love of the has its own unique design, radiating an extraordinary sense of silent power. Guanyin, showering nectar upon sentient beings. Each part of the sculpture

viewingNote.zh: 丹萨替寺位于西藏自治区山南地区桑日县境内，雅鲁藏布江北岸的帕木竹渡口 。 寺院依山而建 ，规模宏大 ，布局合理 。1158 年由噶举派僧人多吉杰布创建 ，建寺之 后，多吉杰布在该寺收徒传法十多年，因其地名叫“帕竹”，该派发展为帕竹噶举 派 ，丹萨替寺遂成帕竹噶举派的主寺。帕竹噶举与政治的关系最为密切，该派曾同朗 氏家族一起建立了政教合一的地方政权 ，并统治卫藏地区达一百三十多年，直到 1481 年才被其属部后藏仁蚌巴推翻 。作为帕竹噶举祖寺的丹萨替寺在 12 至 15 世纪 之间积累了大量的财富，成为了当时藏地闻名的宗教、政治 、文化中心 。 此尊绿度母头顶束髻，发丝盘束有致 ，发髻上以珠宝镶嵌 ，饰以连珠纹 。面庞圆 润，眉间白毫以绿松石镶嵌 ，双眉如钩，弯曲细长 。双目俯视前方，目光柔和 。鼻梁 挺拔 ，小嘴微抿，下颌饱满，神情宁静安详 。度母呈自在坐姿 ，左腿横盘 ，右腿自然 下垂，伸出莲台之外，踩于足莲之上 。左手当胸结说法印，右手掌心向外，置于膝 前，施与愿印，双手各捻一茎连枝 ，连枝自两肩攀缘而上 。 度母宽肩细腰，胸部饱满丰腴 ，腰肢纤细，四肢健美 。耳戴圆形耳珰，上身裸 露，胸前佩戴项链 、璎珞，璎珞的系带于颈后打结，而后自然垂落，帔帛自双肩绕臂 而下，垂落于身体两侧的台座上 。下身着贴体长裙，长裙上有一道道连珠纹隔出的条 状花纹 ，并以各色宝石镶嵌装饰 。腰间系带打结，自腹前垂至台面，绕过脚下，延伸 到台座边缘，系带的褶皱自然流畅，显示出衣物轻柔的质感 。仰覆式束腰莲台，莲瓣 饱满秀长 ，层次丰富，上下对称，周匝环绕 ，莲台上下边缘各有一周连珠纹 ，颗颗饱 满 。封底采用传统的包底法，底板中央有十字金刚杵纹 ，图案刻画规范 。此像莲台 后方没有与灵塔上连接的铜条，作为丹萨梯寺单尊陈列供奉，其鎏金，光彩夺目，身 上镶嵌各色宝石 、绿松石 、水晶以及各色宝石，都为丹萨替寺造像的显著特点 。
viewingNote.en: Densatil Monastery is located in Sangri County, Shannan Prefecture, Tibet Autonomous Region, near the Pe machu Ferry on the northern bank of the Yarlung Tsangpo River. The temple is built against the mountain, - grand in scale and well-arranged. It was founded in 1158 by the Gelug sect monk Dorje Gyalpo, who later spent more than ten years teaching and transmitting Buddhist teachings there. This sect developed into the the Pagdru Kagyu school, with the Densatil Monastery becoming its main temple. This sect had close ties with politics, together with the Lang clan, they established a local theocratic authority, ruling over the Ü-Tsang region for over 130 years until it was overthrown by Rinpungpa, a sub-sect, in 1481. As the ancestral monastery of Pagdru Kagyu sect, the Densatil Monastery amassed a significant amount of wealth between the 12th and 15th centuries, becoming a renowned religious, political, and cultural center in Tibet during that time. Green Tara is portrayed with a topknot adorned with carefully arranged strands of hair, and the topknot is embellished with gemstones and decorated with beaded patterns. Her face is round and smooth, with an urna ornamented with turquoise between her eyebrows. Her arched eyebrows are gracefully curved and slen der. Her gaze is directed forward, and she has a straight nose, a slightly pursed small mouth, a full chin, and a - gently upturned face, creating a serene and composed expression. Green Tara is depicted in a relaxed seated posture, with her left leg crossed and the right leg naturally hanging down, extending beyond the lotus pedestal and resting on a lotus flower. She wears circular ear ornaments and is bare-chested. Around her neck, she wears a necklace and a festoon of pearls, with the ties of the festoon knotted behind her neck and then draping naturally. Her outer garment drapes over her shoulders, wraps around her arms, and extends down on both sides of the pedestal. Her left hand forms the teaching mudra, while her right hand, with the palm facing outward, rests in her lap, making the varada mudra, the gesture of granting wishes. Each hand holds a lotus stem that is intertwined, with the stems extending in various directions, some climbing up behind her and stretching upwards, creating a multi-dimensional space alongside the cascading drapery on either side of her body. Green Tara is depicted with a broad shoulder and a slender waist, with realistic detailing of her abdomen and chest. She wears a form-fitting long robe adorned with rows of beaded patterns, embellished with various colored gemstones. A knotted sash is wrapped around her waist, hanging down from the front to the base, winding around her feet, and extending to the edge of the pedestal. The folds of the sash are naturally flowing, conveying the soft texture of the clothing. She is seated on a double-lotus pedestal, with full and elegant lotus petals arranged symmetrically around the base. Both the upper and lower edges of the pedestal are decorated with rows of beaded patterns, each bead appearing plump and well-defined. The base of the sculpture follows traditional techniques, with a central depiction of a crossed Vajra symbol. This sculpture does not have copper strips connecting it to the linga on top of the pedestal. It is displayed as a single deity in Densatil Monastery and is known for its striking gilded appearance. The arrangement and inlay of various colored gemstones, including turquoise and crystal, are distinctive features of sculptures from Densatil Monastery.

viewingNote.zh: 在西藏佛教雕塑艺术史上，丹萨替造像无疑是一种最具影响的造像风格，它以 完美的造型、雄健的躯体 、繁缛的装饰 、精致的工艺，不仅展现了西藏佛像艺术史上 空前绝后的壮美艺术风范，而且也见证了一个家族 、一个教派的兴衰历史；曾经雄伟 壮丽的丹萨替寺现已失去了往日的光彩，所以这些精美的造像更显得弥足珍贵 。 此尊智慧空行母三面六臂，束高发髻，发丝盘束有致 ，髻顶装饰金刚杵，佩戴骷 髅宝冠，骷髅嘴里吐出的璎珞垂于空行母额前，华丽精美 ，极具装饰性 。宽额丰 颐 ，面部饱满，鼻梁高挺 ，呲牙咧嘴，三目圆瞪 ，杏仁状的大眼使得表情静穆又慈 悲，神情具有强烈的穿透力 。双耳垂肩，佩戴镶嵌了宝石的圆形耳珰 。上身披十字 瑜伽带，并有长蛇在身前及腰间缠束，蛇在藏传佛教中代表贪婪，以蛇为饰代表对 贪欲的降服 。手脚佩戴的臂钏刻画的极为精细，连珠纹颗颗饱满 、分明，穿插关系 明显 。六臂依次探出，三只右手分别持有法杖、金刚斧钺 、金刚杵；左手持颅碗 、金 刚铃。佛像塑造之时，凡遇多臂形象者，手臂间的位置与角度在造型中美感的体现 极为重要，此处的六只手臂分为三层，错落有致 ，在身侧形成两道优雅的扇形，和谐 巧妙，各手指姿态皆有细微变化，有韵律地带给雕塑一种奋发向上的气势。空母行 盘腿而坐 ，腿上有垂落的璎珞，脚掌刻画写实细腻 。 坐骑雄狮匍匐于莲台之上，造型生动，形态逼真 ，似乎正在咆哮怒吼，颈上有 髦，颈下系铃和璎珞，背上垂挂的璎珞均镶嵌宝石，足显富贵；其体态健壮，尤其四 腿肌肉线条饱满圆润，似蓄力欲起身，颇具动势。狮尾竖向弯曲垂于背上，毛发纤屡 皆细刻而出 。狮子身下为单层覆式莲花座 ，莲瓣饱满秀长 ，层次丰富，周匝环绕 。 此尊气势恢宏的智慧空行母像，材质厚重 ，整像造形完美 ，比例精准，结构复杂，人 物敦实饱满，金色醇厚，镶嵌华丽灿然 ，应为丹萨替寺鼎盛时期的作品 。
viewingNote.en: In the history of Tibetan Buddhist sculpture art, the Densatil Monastery style undoubtedly stands as one of the most influential. It is characterized by its perfect form, robust physique, intricate decorations, and exquisite craftsmanship. Not only does it showcase an unparalleled artistic grandeur in the history, but it also bears witness to the vicissitudes of a lineage and a religious tradition. The once magnificent Densatil Monastery has now lost its former glory, making these exquisite sculptures all the more precious. Jnana Dakini has three faces and six arms and an elaborately adorned high topknot with hair neatly coiled. At the top of the topknot, there is a vajra scepter ornament. She wears a crown adorned with a skull, from which the festoons of pearls hanging from it drapes over her forehead. This ornamentation is exquisitely de tailed with effective decorative design. She has a broad forehead, full cheeks, a high-bridged nose, and a fierce - expression with bared teeth. Her three eyes are round and piercing, and her almond-shaped large eyes give her a serene yet compassionate expression, radiating a powerful presence. Her ears hang down to her shoulders, adorned with circular ear pendants encrusted with gemstones. The upper body is adorned with a crossed vajra belt, and a long snake is coiled around the body and waist, symbolizing the subjugation of greed, as snakes represent greed in Tibetan Buddhism. The armlets worn on her arms and legs are finely detailed, with each bead in the beaded pattern being full and distinct, and the interlocking relationship between them is clearly evident. The sculpture has six arms, and from right to left, they hold a katvanga staff, an axe, and a vajra scepter in the right hands, while the left hands hold a skullcup and a bell. In the progress of sculpting a Buddha, especially one with multiple arms, the positioning and angles of the arms play a crucial role in the aesthetic expression. In this case, the six arms are divided into three layers, gracefully arranged by her sides, forming two elegant fan-shaped patterns. The positioning is ingenious and harmonious with subtle variations in the gestures of each hand, lending a consistent space effect that imparts a positive and determined energy to the sculpture. She sits in a cross-legged posture with festoons hanging down on her legs, and the depiction of her feet is vivid and intricate. Her mount, a strong lion, crouches on top of a lotus pedestal, depicted with vivid and lifelike details. It seems to be roaring with a mane on its neck. Hanging from its neck are bells and necklaces, while jeweled festoons adorn its back, symbolizing opulence and magnificent. The lion’s physique is robust, especially the muscular contours of its four legs, which appear full and rounded, as if it’s gathering strength to rise, conveying a sense of dynamic energy. The lion’s tail curves upward and hangs gracefully on its back with its delicate fur meticulously carved. Beneath the lion, there’s a single-layer, inverted lotus pedestal with full and elegant petals, displaying rich pieces that surround it. This magnificent sculpture of the Jnana Dakini, with its substantial material, perfect form, precise proportions, intricate structure, robust figure, rich golden hue, and splendid inlay work, is believed to be a product of the heyday of the Densatil Monastery

description.zh: 2024 年 8 月 15 日，星期四，成都又一个雨天，又一次在雨声中找到了灵感，又一 次为展览图录撰写序言。 从业近十年，每一年都怀着谦卑的心态去学习与体悟；今年是做公众展的第三 年，图录也是第三本了，尽管每年编辑展览作品成册的过程并不容易，但一想到能让 更多的人走近，并感受到西藏艺术及喜马拉雅艺术品所蕴藏的巨大能量，我们却依旧 乐此不疲。 于我而言，西藏艺术及喜马拉雅艺术品不仅是物质的展示，更是精神的体验。它 们像古老的藏经，富含哲思与智慧。每件作品背后都承载着丰富的文化传承和生活哲 学，教会我敬畏自然、尊重生灵，并在喧嚣中寻找平静。 喜马拉雅艺术对许多人来说可能陌生而神秘，如同十年前的我；但这也正是这次 展览的意义所在——打破界限！让更多人了解这一伟大的艺术传统。我们希望通过 展览激发大家对西藏艺术及喜马拉雅艺术的兴趣，并在文化中体悟那些古老的智 慧、在匠人精神中感受时间的从容、在艺术的世界中重塑思辨力、在精神的世界中寻 找到灵魂的慰藉 ... 此次展览我们甄选了 38 件展品，其中包括了佛像、法器、唐卡、杂项，包含了金 铜、木雕、石雕、陶质、夹竺等不同材质与工艺。展品的年份从公元 2-3 世纪到 18 世 纪 , 跨越 1700 余年，产地风格分别含盖了印度、尼泊尔、中国西藏、北京、蒙古。这 些艺术品不仅代表了独特的宗教信仰和艺术传统，还讲述了千百年来在那片土地上生 生不息的人文故事。通过这些展品，我们可以感受到来自雪山之巅的精神力量，以及 人们在高原上与自然、神灵和自我之间的和谐对话。 窗外的雨下得酣畅淋漓，就像我酒杯中的威士忌，猛烈而尽兴！雨在浇灭酷暑的 同时也点燃了生命；酒在刺激着味蕾的瞬间也激发着灵感… 西藏艺术及喜马拉雅艺 术品的浩海，召唤着我们在生命之舟上挂起美的风帆！海阔、天空，我们是如此脆弱 却又如此刚毅；山高、路长，我们是如此细微却又这般宏伟…
description.en: August 15, 2024，Thursday, another rainy day in Chengdu. Once again, as I write this preface for the exhibition catalogue, I find myself inspired by the sound of the rain. Having spent nearly a decade in this field, I continue to approach each year with humility, eager to learn and reflect. This marks the third year of presenting a public exhibition, as well as the creation of our third catalog. Although compiling these works into a book is no easy task, the idea of bringing more people closer to the immense energy encapsulated in Xizang and Himalayan art makes every ef￾fort worthwhile. As for me, Xizang and Himalayan art is a spiritual encounter rather than a mere physical display. These works are like ancient Xizang scriptures, rich with philosophical thoughts and timeless wis￾dom. Each piece carries within it deep cultural heritage and profound philosophies of life , teaching reverence for nature, respect for all living beings, and the pursuit of tranquility amidst the chaos. For many, Himalayan art may seem distant and mysterious, as it once appeared to me a decade ago. Yet, this is precisely the purpose of this exhibition—to break down those barriers! To bring this ex￾traordinary artistic tradition into the lives of many. Our hope is that the exhibition will ignite a curiosity for Xizang and Himalayan art, leading visitors to explore the ancient wisdom within the culture, to feel the passage of time through the hands of masterful artisans, to deepen their reflective capacity through art, and to solace for the soul in its spiritual depth. This exhibition showcases 38 carefully selected pieces, including Buddha statues, ritual objects, thangkas, and various other items. These artifacts span a wide array of materials and techniques, from gilt bronze and wood carving to stone carving, ceramics, and papier-mâché. The exhibits span from the 2nd to the 18th century, covering over 1,700 years, and originate from India, Nepal, Xizang, Beijing, and Mongolia. These works not only reflect unique religious beliefs and artistic traditions but also tell the enduring human stories of those who lived on these lands. Through these pieces, we can sense the spiritual forces rising from the peaks of the Himalayas, and the harmonious conversations between the people of the plateau and nature, deities, and the self. Outside, the rain pours down with vigor, much like the whiskey in my glass—fierce, yet satisfying. The rain, while quenching the heat, breathes life anew; the whiskey, as it stirs the senses, sparks creativ￾ity. The vast ocean of Xizang and Himalayan art beckons us to set sail on the vessel of beauty. The hori￾zon stretches wide, the sky infinite—we are fragile, yet so resilient. The mountains tower high, the path winds long—we are but fleeting moments in time, yet magnificent in our voyage.

intro.zh: 原始 、壮阔 、苍茫 、野性 、韧劲……

description.zh: 绵延的喜马拉雅山脉既是俯视众生的存在 ，也是诸 多世界性的宗教 、艺术的源头 ！ 纯净的雪域高原滋养着 让我着迷的人文 、艺术 ，每当想起那些奔放不羁的线 条 、炽热绚烂的色彩 ，总会让我们明白人与自然的和谐 共生 、秩序与自由的博弈 、平衡 ！由同样元素构成的大千世界 ，在那里却 分 外 地 线 条 分 明 、色 彩 澄 净 ！ 更 重 要 的 是 ，如此美妙的雪域高原 、阳光之城背后所蕴 藏的是有关生命的感性 ，有关世界的哲思 ！ 在这个层面上 ，这片土地对所有人都不陌 生 ，能给所有人带来那种回归本真 、内省自身 的熟悉感 ；在这里 ，我们和天地间的一切产 生了联系 ，自然和文化 、物质和精神形成了融 合——也只有如此才能产生令人惊叹的美 感 ，才能诞生震憾人心的视觉冲击 ；在灵性 和本性的底色之上 ，浓墨重彩的涂抹着一种 原始的精神之美；与内心深处的质朴情感共 振出了真我意识 ... 这一切使得这里的每一个 角落都浩翰连宇 ，每一寸土地都神秘珍贵 。以文载道 ，以美化人 ！ 文化初始 ，从象征性符号到具象人物 雕塑的出现 ，人们探寻着对美的理想表达 。文明的碰撞 、思 想的沉淀 ，产生了对世界的笃定 ，发展出了文化上的自信 。 喜马拉雅山脉周边的不同地区尝试着用不同的雕塑语言 、形 态表达着他们的情感和故事 ，丰富的艺术形式无不展示着他 们的才华和审美感知 ，并在相互碰撞之间产生了震憾世界的 作品 。
description.en: The Himalayas, which roll ridge upon ridge, tower over all creatures like a divinity who bestowed upon humanity many of the religions and arts now practised worldwide. The pristine snow-covered plateau is home to charming cultures and arts characterized by unrestrained lines and bright color, which are evidence of the harmonious co-existence of human and nature and the dynamic balance of order and freedom.It is extraordinary that the Himalayas with the same composition of chemical elements as the rest of world have such clarity and purity! More importantly, this snowy place bathed in sunshine can inspire the sensibility of life and reflection of the world. In this sense, there is no stranger on this land, where even newcomers feel at home as they restore their real selves and turn their thoughts inward. As the line blurs between nature and culture as well as the objective reality and the mental world, one feels connected with everything on earth, his or her breath taken away and vision struck by the beauty of nature. Here the primitive beauty of spirit is mixed with human vitality and nature and interacts with one’s simple feelings deep down to raise awareness of the “authentic self” . All of these factors make every corner of this land a precious and mysterious universe. Culture is intended to drive truths and beauty to enlighten people. Since the emergence of culture, humans have tried to express beauty in many ways, from symbolic signs to figure statues. With the collision of civilizations and accumulation of wisdom, they finally gained a foothold in the world and developed confidence in their own cultures. In different areas around the Himalayas, people use different forms and “languages” of sculpture to express their emotions and tell their stories. While competing with each other, the rich forms of art—the crystallization of the people’s talent and aesthetics—have given rise to marvelous works that astound the world.

title.zh: 为什么我们持续做展览与图录
title.en: Why We Continue to Make Exhibitions and Catalogues

excerpt.zh: 展览不是销售陈列的延伸，而是把判断公开、把研究组织成可被观看的方式。
excerpt.en: An exhibition is not an extension of display for sale, but a way of making judgement public and research visible.

body.zh: 对古代艺术而言，展览的意义不只是集中呈现作品，更在于建立清晰的观看结构，让作品之间形成可比较、可讨论的关系。
body.en: For historical art, the value of an exhibition lies not only in gathering works, but in creating a clear structure of viewing through which works can be compared and discussed.

body.zh: 图录则承担另一层功能，它把现场经验沉淀为可检索、可引用的文字与图像档案。
body.en: The catalogue serves another purpose, condensing the exhibition experience into searchable, citable texts and images.

body.zh: 竹瑾居把展览与图录视为品牌工作的核心，因为它们直接决定专业判断是否能够被长期保存与传递。
body.en: Zhu Jin Ju treats exhibitions and catalogues as central to its practice because they directly determine whether professional judgement can be preserved and transmitted over time.

description.zh: 竹瑾居不追求门类堆叠，而是在少数真正重要的方向上持续积累判断、图像档案与展览经验。
description.en: Zhu Jin Ju does not pursue breadth for its own sake, but builds judgement, image archives, and exhibition experience within a few important fields.

title.zh: 由展览、图录与研究累积出的公开方法
title.en: A Public Method Built Through Exhibitions, Catalogues, and Research

description.zh: 对藏家、机构与研究者而言，可信赖并不来自夸张表达，而来自判断的一致性、公开资料的清晰度与持续积累。
description.en: For collectors, institutions, and researchers, trust comes not from spectacle but from consistent judgement, clear public documentation, and sustained work.

title.zh: 欢迎藏家、机构、策展人与研究者联系。
title.en: Collectors, Institutions, Curators, and Researchers Are Welcome.

description.zh: 如需咨询具体作品、专题合作、机构借展、图录交换或研究交流，可通过联系页面提交信息。
description.en: For inquiries about individual works, collaborations, institutional loans, catalogue exchange, or research discussion, please use the contact page.
